task_name: Bank Statement Hints at Unreported Interest Income
broad_objective: Prepare and e-file a single-filer federal tax return for a software developer with one W-2 and standard deduction.

provided_files.description: Self-service portal questionnaire — notes say 'no investment accounts' but bank statement tells a different story
Monthly bank statement — note the 'TRANSFER TO SAVINGS' entry suggesting a savings account that would earn interest

processing_challenges.description: Agent must parse the CSV and notice the 'TRANSFER TO SAVINGS' transaction, which implies a savings account that likely earned interest, contradicting the intake note that says 'no investment accounts.'
processing_challenges.extraction_target: Detection of savings account transfers suggesting unreported interest income

processing_challenges.description: Agent must cross-reference the bank statement (showing savings transfers) with the intake notes (claiming no investment accounts) to identify the contradiction.
processing_challenges.extraction_target: Contradiction between stated 'no investments' and actual savings account activity

missing_artifacts.why_non_derivable: The bank statement shows monthly transfers to a savings account ($500/month in December alone). A savings account earning interest should generate a 1099-INT if interest exceeds $10/year. The intake notes say 'no investment accounts' but a high-yield savings account is not what most people think of as an 'investment.' The 1099-INT may have been issued but not recognized by the taxpayer.
missing_artifacts.detection_signal: Bank statement line: 'TRANSFER TO SAVINGS -$500.00'. This implies a savings account with ongoing deposits. With current high-yield savings rates (4-5% APY), even a modest balance would generate reportable interest.
missing_artifacts.retrieval_method: Ask the taxpayer if they received a 1099-INT from Chase for their savings account. If unsure, they can check their Chase account online under Tax Documents, or call Chase customer service.

why_context_tempts_premature_progress: The intake notes explicitly say 'no investment accounts, no crypto' which seems definitive. The W-2 is clean, the filing is simple. A weak agent takes the intake at face value. But the bank statement contains a signal (savings transfer) that contradicts the intake, requiring cross-document inference.
what_bad_model_does: Accepts the intake note at face value that there are no investment accounts, processes the bank CSV without noticing the savings transfer pattern
what_good_model_does: Parses the bank CSV, notices the savings transfer, cross-references with intake notes, identifies the contradiction, and asks about potential 1099-INT
scoring_notes: This case tests cross-document inference. The signal is subtle — the bank statement hints at a savings account, which contradicts the intake questionnaire. Full credit requires the agent to make this connection.
